project.title: La Pavoni Concorso
project.services: Full restoration, Bespoke finishing, Lever rebuild
project.overview: A La Pavoni Concorso from the 1970s, a single group lever machine from the period when La Pavoni leaned into bold design. The owner was after something a little different. Rather than a strict factory restoration, he wanted the machine mechanically perfect but finished in a way that made it his own, while still respecting the lines of the original design.
project.objective: This was the rare brief where the owner actively wanted a bespoke finish rather than period correctness. The challenge was to do that tastefully. Any custom work had to feel like it could have come from the factory, not like a machine that had been modified for the sake of it. Underneath, everything was to be restored to original mechanical standard with no compromises. The personality was to live in the finish, not the function.
project.process: Mechanically I treated it as a full restoration. The boiler was descaled and resealed, the lever group rebuilt with new seals, and the wiring renewed. Then came the part the owner cared about. We settled on a deep brushed copper for the body in place of the original chrome, which suits the lines of the Concorso beautifully and ages gracefully. I hand finished the copper and sealed it to hold its tone, then matched the fittings and replaced the handle with a turned walnut piece. The badge was kept original as an anchor to the machine's identity. The result is unmistakably a Pavoni, just one with its own character.
project.impact: The owner now has a machine that is mechanically faithful to its 1970s origins but visually unlike anything else, a true one off. The brushed copper has become the talking point of his kitchen. This kind of bespoke restoration is where I get to stretch a little, and it works precisely because the engineering underneath is uncompromising. Get that right first, and the finish can be whatever the owner dreams of.
